weekly_overview.raw_line: What to watch during the week of Monday 4/20 - the key macro events this week include US retail sales for Mar (Tues 4/21 at 8:30am ET), Warsh’s Fed confirmation hearing (Tues 4/21), and the US flash PMIs for April (Thurs 4/23 at 9:45am ET). The Fed will be in a quiet period ahead of its decision on 4/29 . The key US earnings reports include: Mon morning ( CLF ); Mon night ( STLD and ZION ); Tues morning ( DGX, DHI, DHR, GE, HAL, MMM, NOC, RTX , and UNH ); Tues night ( CB, COF, ISRG , and UAL ); Wed morning ( BA, BSX, ELV, GEV, MAS, MCO, OTIS, PM, T, TEL , and VRT ); Wed night ( CCI, CSX, IBM, KNX, LRCX, LUV, LVS, NOW, TSLA, TXN , and URI ); Thurs morning ( AAL, AXP, BX, CMCSA, DOW, FCX, HON, KDP, LMT, PHM, TMO , and UNP ); Thurs night ( BKR, INTC, NEM , and SAP ); and Fri morning ( CHTR, HCA, NSC, PG , and SLB ). For European earnings , the key reports include: Wed morning ( ABB, Akzo Nobel, Danone , and Reckitt Benckiser ) and Thurs morning ( BE Semiconductor, Heineken, LSEG, Man Group, Nestle, Nokia, Roche, Sanofi, Schindler , and STM ).

The Fed will be in a quiet period ahead of its decision on 4/29 . The key US earnings reports include: Mon morning ( CLF ); Mon night ( STLD and ZION ); Tues morning ( DGX, DHI, DHR, GE, HAL, MMM, NOC, RTX , and UNH ); Tues night ( CB, COF, ISRG , and UAL ); Wed morning ( BA, BSX, ELV, GEV, MAS, MCO, OTIS, PM, T, TEL , and VRT ); Wed night ( CCI, CSX, IBM, KNX, LRCX, LUV, LVS, NOW, TSLA, TXN , and URI ); Thurs morning ( AAL, AXP, BX, CMCSA, DOW, FCX, HON, KDP, LMT, PHM, TMO , and UNP ); Thurs night ( BKR, INTC, NEM , and SAP ); and Fri morning ( CHTR, HCA, NSC, PG , and SLB ). For European earnings , the key reports include: Wed morning ( ABB, Akzo Nobel, Danone , and Reckitt Benckiser ) and Thurs morning ( BE Semiconductor, Heineken, LSEG, Man Group, Nestle, Nokia, Roche, Sanofi, Schindler , and STM ).

source_subject: Vital Knowledge: Vital Dawn for Friday April 17, 2026
market_levels:
Market levels – the US equity futures are ticking higher so far this morning: S&P +12.7 points/18bp, Dow +150 points/31bp, Nasdaq +30 points/11bp, and R2 K +10.4 points/38bp. NFLX is down ~9.8% in pre-market trading. The major Eurozone indices are up 25-50bp, with outperformance in luxury, tech, and media while utilities, energy, telecom, basic resources, and autos underperform . Alstom is tumbling nearly 30% on a neg. earnings update while Norsk Hydro falls on the underwhelming Alcoa report Thurs night. Tech is the top group in Europe and is being led higher by software/services . Stocks in Asia mostly suffered losses on Friday: TPX -1.41%, NKY -1.75%, Hang Seng -0.89%, HSCEI -0.67%, SHCOMP -0.1%, Shenzhen +0.37%, Taiwan TAIEX -0.88%, Korea KOSPI -0.55%, Australia ASX 200 -0.09%, and India is up 45-50bp. Treasuries aren’t doing much (yields are flat-to-down 1bp across the curve). The DXY is dipping 13bp. Brent is sliding 200bp (to ~$97.40) although remains up on WTD basis. Gold is about flat while silver climbs 150bp. Bitcoin is advancing 90bp.
•
whats_happening:
What’s happening this morning – there wasn’t anything super interesting out since the Thurs close, although Trump’s Iran rhetoric is becoming increasingly bullish/hopeful (he said Tehran agreed to hand over its enriched uranium and abandon plans for a nuclear weapon while Pakistani sources tell Reuters a MOU between the US and Iran could be signed soon ). We remain very confident that the US-Iran will stay on a path of deescalation , but as this is now the consensus perspective , resolution of the conflict won’t be as potent a source of upside risk as has been the case so far this month . Only a handful of firms had earnings out Thurs night/Fri morning, and results were generally underwhelming , at least on the surface, including AA, Alstom, Ericsson, KNX , and NFLX (although the overall KNX update was net positive , the latest encouraging data point from the trucking industry, and we would place NFLX more in the “inline” bucket rather than the “disappointing” one ).
• What we’ll be watching on Friday – the focus will be on earnings before the US open ( ALLY, ALV, FITB, RF, STT , and TFC ).
• What we’ll be watching over the weekend – Trump said the US and Iran could meet face-to-face again over the weekend , although nothing has been officially confirmed .
• What we’ll be watching during the week of Monday 4/20 – the main (scheduled) macro events will be Warsh’s confirmation hearing (Tues 4/21) and the flash PMIs for Apr (Thurs 4/23). Otherwise, the focus will be on earnings and while there are a ton of names scheduled , we care most about AXP (Thurs morning), BX (Thurs morning), GEV (Wed morning), IBM (Wed night), INTC (Thurs night), LRCX (Wed night), NOW (Wed night), PG (Fri morning), SAP (Thurs night), TEL (Wed morning), TSLA (Wed night), TXN (Wed night), and VRT (Wed morning).
• Market in a Minute (macro) – Trump expressed optimism about an Iran deal , claiming Tehran agreed to hand over its enriched uranium and abandon plans for a nuclear weapon (“I think something’s going to happen, very positive, very important…I think we’re very close to making a deal with Iran”) while Pakistani sources said diplomacy was yielding progress and that a MOU could soon be signed between the two sides ( Washington Post , Reuters ); Oil tankers are rushing to the US from around the world to buy American energy products amid ongoing disruption in the Middle East ( Nikkei ); UK’s Starmer faces fresh calls to resign over the Mandelson scandal ( FT ); US gov’t at risk of losing its status as the lowest-cost USD borrower as global investors grow wary of Trump’s volatility ( FT )
• Market in a Minute (micro – Friday morning) – AAPL (Apple) saw i Phone shipments in China spike 20% in Q1 according to Counterpoint Research ( Reuters ); Alstom shares collapse in European trading after the company a mixed earnings update, with strong orders/demand but poor execution, which hurt margins (adjusted margins were 6%, about 100bp below guidance), and the outlook for margins/cash flow was weak (mgmt. is withdrawing the prior 3-year free cash flow goal of EU1.5 B and won’t meet its 8-10% margin goal by F26/27) ( Alstom ); Data center delays : data center construction in the US is falling behind schedule due to opposition from local governments along with shortages (of labor, power, and equipment) ( FT ); Ericsson reported a shortfall in Q1 sales (organic growth of +6% was about 180bp below the consensus due to weakness in the Americas) and EBITA margins (11.3% vs. the Street 11.5%), and the company acknowledges “geopolitical and macroeconomic uncertainties” along with cost pressures (“We are facing increasing input costs, especially in semiconductors, caused in part by AI demand”) ( Ericsson )
• Market in a Minute (micro – Thursday night) - AA (Alcoa) reported a shortfall in Q1 on EPS (1.40 vs. the Street 1.57), EBITDA ($595 MM vs. the Street $665 MM), and sales ($3.193 B vs. the Street $3.26 B), w/mgmt. calling out headwinds from the Middle East war and Cyclone Narelle; KNX (Knight-Swift) warned that EPS in Q1 will come in at 9c (a large miss vs. the Street’s 26c forecast) due to a number of headwinds (the company calls out ~20c worth of items in the press release), but the Q2 guidance is essentially inline (they see EPS in Q2 of 45-49c vs. the Street 48c) and mgmt.’s qualitative commentary is bullish on the macro landscape; NFLX (Netflix) reported modest sales upside in Q1 (+16.2% to $12.25 B vs. the Street $12.17 B, w/growth driven by membership increases, higher prices, and increased ad revenue) w/very robust free cash flow ($5.094 B vs. the Street $2.66 B, w/the beat driven in large part by the WBD termination fee), but op. margins fell slightly short (32.3% vs. the Street 32.4%), and while the prior full-year guidance is reiterated (for sales and op. margins), the Q2 forecast is below expectations; NI (Ni Source) announced a new long‑term energy agreement with Google that will support the development and operation of a large‑scale data center in northern Indiana; ON (On Semi) said helium supplies won’t be a major issue for it this year despite disruptions in the Middle East ( Semafor ); Open AI will spend >$20 B on AI chips from Cerebras and is set to receive warrants as part of the deal ( The Information )
• What to watch during the week of Monday 4/20 – the key macro events this week include US retail sales for Mar (Tues 4/21 at 8:30am ET), Warsh’s Fed confirmation hearing (Tues 4/21), and the US flash PMIs for April (Thurs 4/23 at 9:45am ET). The Fed will be in a quiet period ahead of its decision on 4/29 . The key US earnings reports include: Mon morning ( CLF ); Mon night ( STLD and ZION ); Tues morning ( DGX, DHI, DHR, GE, HAL, MMM, NOC, RTX , and UNH ); Tues night ( CB, COF, ISRG , and UAL ); Wed morning ( BA, BSX, ELV, GEV, MAS, MCO, OTIS, PM, T, TEL , and VRT ); Wed night ( CCI, CSX, IBM, KNX, LRCX, LUV, LVS, NOW, TSLA, TXN , and URI ); Thurs morning ( AAL, AXP, BX, CMCSA, DOW, FCX, HON, KDP, LMT, PHM, TMO , and UNP ); Thurs night ( BKR, INTC, NEM , and SAP ); and Fri morning ( CHTR, HCA, NSC, PG , and SLB ). For European earnings , the key reports include: Wed morning ( ABB, Akzo Nobel, Danone , and Reckitt Benckiser ) and Thurs morning ( BE Semiconductor, Heineken, LSEG, Man Group, Nestle, Nokia, Roche, Sanofi, Schindler , and STM ).

Iran

• What bulls are talking about : nearly all of the headlines out of the Middle East point to an easing of acrimony and tensions as the US and Iran head toward an extension of the current ceasefire and an eventual nuclear/Hormuz deal . Trump said the war “should be ending pretty soon” and claimed that Iran has agreed to give up its enriched uranium and abandoned ambitions to build a nuclear weapon . In addition, Israel and Lebanon will observe a 10-day ceasefire.
• What bears are talking about : it’s now overwhelmingly the consensus view that the US and Iran will reach some sort of a deal to resolve their conflict and reopen Hormuz , which means headlines pointing to an agreement won’t place as much upside pressure on stocks as they did before (and the fallout from more acrimony will be significant). A durable détente isn’t guaranteed – Washington and Tehran still have some big differences to iron out , as do Israel and Lebanon . According to a Bloomberg report on Thurs, some Gulf and European governments believe it will take the US and Iran about 6 months to reach a deal , a far longer timeframe than is suggested by the current market narrative. A Reuters article highlighted the negative impact to US munitions supplies from the war , which will force the administration to delay certain weapons deliveries to Europe . Even if a deal is reached , it won’t erase the fallout from the war – the stagflationary effects of Epic Fury will be with markets for a while. The big price increase announced from PPG Wed night won’t be an isolated incident – companies across the board will be forced to adjust pricing given what’s happening in commodities, and ~$80-95 is likely to be the “new normal” for Brent vs. ~$60-70 before the war.

International macro

• BOJ’s Ueda’s remarks make no hint at Apr rate hike, reducing the chances of tightening later this month ( Reuters )
• Europe is set to dramatically relax its M&A rules in an effort to create more “global champions” capable of competing on a global scale ( FT )
• Germany’s gov’t cut the country’s growth forecasts for ’26 and ’27 ( Reuters )
• Japanese workers secure healthy wage increases , keeping the BOJ on a tightening trajectory ( Bloomberg )
• UK’s Starmer faces fresh calls to resign over the Mandelson scandal ( FT )
• Venezuela : the IMF will resume formal contact with the country ( Bloomberg )

US macro

• US gov’t at risk of losing its status as the lowest-cost USD borrower as global investors grow wary of Trump’s volatility ( FT )
• The US economy continues to grapple with the Big 3 supply shocks of the Trump 2.0 era (tariffs, immigration, and Iran). Bessent earlier in the week said tariff rates should be back to pre-SCOTUS levels by the summer . The Fed isn’t likely to provide much support as elevated inflation keeps the Funds Rate unchanged for the foreseeable future while the pace of asset purchases is set to slump significantly (and Warsh’s long-term plans for balance sheet shrinkage remain an overhang).

Washington

• Oil tankers are rushing to the US from around the world to buy American energy products amid ongoing disruption in the Middle East ( Nikkei )
• White House officials urge the oil/gas industry to increase output to help offset the Iran war fallout ( Bloomberg )
• Oil and gas executives urge the White House not to allow Iran to charge a toll on Hormuz traffic ( FT )
• White House will begin attempting to collect delinquent student loans as soon as this summer ( Politico )
• Trump is considering appointing Michael Murray to lead antitrust at the DOJ ( Bloomberg )
• Trump plans to nominate Cameron Hamilton to run FEMA (he was pushed out as acting leader nearly a year ago) ( Politico )
• Trump will nominate Dr. Erica Schwartz to lead CDC (she was the deputy surgeon general during Trump’s first term) ( NBC News )
• US school chronic absenteeism remains extremely elevated at nearly 25% ( FT )
• Florida’s lack of private schools has prompted some billionaires to build their own ( WSJ )

Financials

• Banks face growing cybersecurity threats from sophisticated new AI models like Anthropic’s Claude Mythos ( FT )

Industrials

• AA (Alcoa) reported a shortfall in Q1 on EPS (1.40 vs. the Street 1.57), EBITDA ($595 MM vs. the Street $665 MM), and sales ($3.193 B vs. the Street $3.26 B), w/mgmt. calling out headwinds from the Middle East war and Cyclone Narelle . The prior full-year guidance for aluminum and alumina production and shipments is unchanged .
• Alstom shares collapse in European trading after the company a mixed earnings update , with strong orders/demand but poor execution , which hurt margins (adjusted margins were 6%, about 100bp below guidance), and the outlook for margins/cash flow was weak (mgmt. is withdrawing the prior 3-year free cash flow goal of EU1.5 B and won’t meet its 8-10% margin goal by F26/27) ( Alstom )
• BA (Boeing) is hiring factory workers at the fastest pace in years as it looks to increase production and replace retirees ( Reuters )
• Gerresheimer has rejected a takeout bid from SLGN (Silgan) , and talks are no longer occurring between the two firms ( Reuters )
• KNX (Knight-Swift) warned that EPS in Q1 will come in at 9c (a large miss vs. the Street’s 26c forecast) due to a number of headwinds (the company calls out ~20c worth of items in the press release), but the Q2 guidance is essentially inline (they see EPS in Q2 of 45-49c vs. the Street 48c), and the macro qualitative commentary is bullish . Mgmt. said freight conditions improved exiting Q1 (they echo what JBHT said Wed night about the TL market tightening because of reduced capacity). “All things considered, we are more optimistic about the earnings opportunity for our businesses over the next several quarters than we were three months ago”. Bottom Line : the Q1 EPS update looks bad, but the overall message from KNX is net positive, and it seems like the trucking industry is evolving for the better thanks to a sharp reduction in capacity.
• Kone is in advanced talks to buy TK Elevator in a deal that could be worth EU25 B ( Bloomberg )

M&A/Strategic Actions

• Europe is set to dramatically relax its M&A rules in an effort to create more “global champions” capable of competing on a global scale ( FT )
• Gerresheimer has rejected a takeout bid from SLGN (Silgan) , and talks are no longer occurring between the two firms ( Reuters )
• Jio Platforms could file paperwork for an IPO next month ( Bloomberg )
• Kone is in advanced talks to buy TK Elevator in a deal that could be worth EU25 B ( Bloomberg )
• Nippon Express Holdings will buy Metro Supply Chain for ~$1.25 B ( Nikkei )
• Only Fans is nearing a deal to sell a minority stake at a valuation of >$3 B ( FT )
• Open AI : a WSJ profile article raises questions about Sam Altman’s outside investment interests and whether they conflict with his role as Open AI’s CEO ( WSJ )
• SFR , the French telecoms group owned by Patrick Drahi, receives a EU20.4 B takeout bid from a consortium consisting of Bouygues, Orange, and Iliad ( FT )
• UBER (Uber) will buy a ~4.5% stake in Delivery Hero from Prosus for EU20/shr. (a 22% premium to Delivery Hero’s average share price for the last month) ( WSJ )

TMT

• AAPL (Apple) saw i Phone shipments in China spike 20% in Q1 according to Counterpoint Research ( Reuters )
• Anthropic – the Trump administration is working to make Mythos available to federal agencies so they can reduce cybersecurity risks ( Reuters )
• Data center delays : data center construction in the US is falling behind schedule due to opposition from local governments along with shortages (of labor, power, and equipment) ( FT )
• Ericsson reported a shortfall in Q1 sales (organic growth of +6% was about 180bp below the consensus due to weakness in the Americas) and EBITA margins (11.3% vs. the Street 11.5%), and the company acknowledges “ geopolitical and macroeconomic uncertainties ” along with cost pressures (“We are facing increasing input costs, especially in semiconductors, caused in part by AI demand”) ( Ericsson )
• GOOGL (Google) is in talks w/the Pentagon to deploy the Gemini model in classified settings ( The Information )
• META (Meta) is boosting the price of its VR headsets because of elevated memory expenses ( Bloomberg )
• NFLX (Netflix) reported modest sales upside in Q1 (+16.2% to $12.25 B vs. the Street $12.17 B, w/growth driven by membership increases, higher prices , and increased ad revenue ) w/ very robust free cash flow ($5.094 B vs. the Street $2.66 B, w/the beat driven in large part by the WBD termination fee), but op. margins fell slightly short (32.3% vs. the Street 32.4%), and while the prior full-year guidance is reiterated (for sales and op. margins), the Q2 forecast is below expectations (they see sales of $12.57 B vs. the Street $12.64 B w/op. margins of 32.6% vs. the Street 34.4%; the op. margin miss in Q2 is a function of higher content amortization, but mgmt. says Q2 should be the peak for this cost headwind in terms of Y/Y growth). Mgmt. said the recent price increases “have gone well” while advertising revenue is on track to hit $3 B in 2026, up 2x Y/Y. The firm will soon launch a redesigned mobile experience , including vertical video . In addition to earnings, the company said Reed Hastings will step down from the board in June to focus on his philanthropy and other pursuits. Bottom Line : this isn’t an awful report by any means (Q1 sales were ahead of plan, Q1 margins didn’t miss by that much, and the full-year guide is reiterated) and the Q2 guidance shortfall seems mostly just an issue of mis-modeling on content amortization as opposed to a sudden softening in business momentum.
• NI (Ni Source) announced a new long‑term energy agreement with Google that will support the development and operation of a large‑scale data center in northern Indiana. Ni Source also announced an expanded agreement with Amazon .
• ON (On Semi) said helium supplies won’t be a major issue for it this year despite disruptions in the Middle East ( Semafor )
• Only Fans is nearing a deal to sell a minority stake at a valuation of >$3 B ( FT )
• Open AI will spend >$20 B on AI chips from Cerebras and is set to receive warrants as part of the deal ( The Information )
• Open AI : a WSJ profile article raises questions about Sam Altman’s outside investment interests and whether they conflict with his role as Open AI’s CEO ( WSJ )
• Open AI on Thurs released a new AI model designed to help with the drug discovery process ( Bloomberg )
• SFR , the French telecoms group owned by Patrick Drahi, receives a EU20.4 B takeout bid from a consortium consisting of Bouygues, Orange, and Iliad ( FT )
• UBER (Uber) will buy a ~4.5% stake in Delivery Hero from Prosus for EU20/shr. (a 22% premium to Delivery Hero’s average share price for the last month) ( WSJ )

Calendar for the week of Monday April 20

• What to watch during the week of Monday 4/20 – the key macro events this week include US retail sales for Mar (Tues 4/21 at 8:30am ET), Warsh’s Fed confirmation hearing (Tues 4/21), and the US flash PMIs for April (Thurs 4/23 at 9:45am ET). The Fed will be in a quiet period ahead of its decision on 4/29 . iran_section:
Iran rhetoric is becoming increasingly bullish/hopeful (he said Tehran agreed to hand over its enriched uranium and abandon plans for a nuclear weapon while Pakistani sources tell Reuters a MOU between the US and Iran could be signed soon ). We remain very confident that the US-Iran will stay on a path of deescalation , but as this is now the consensus perspective , resolution of the conflict won’t be as potent a source of upside risk as has been the case so far this month . Only a handful of firms had earnings out Thurs night/Fri morning, and results were generally underwhelming , at least on the surface, including AA, Alstom, Ericsson, KNX , and NFLX (although the overall KNX update was net positive , the latest encouraging data point from the trucking industry, and we would place NFLX more in the “inline” bucket rather than the “disappointing” one ).
• What we’ll be watching on Friday – the focus will be on earnings before the US open ( ALLY, ALV, FITB, RF, STT , and TFC ).
• What we’ll be watching over the weekend – Trump said the US and Iran could meet face-to-face again over the weekend , although nothing has been officially confirmed .
• What we’ll be watching during the week of Monday 4/20 – the main (scheduled) macro events will be Warsh’s confirmation hearing (Tues 4/21) and the flash PMIs for Apr (Thurs 4/23). Otherwise, the focus will be on earnings and while there are a ton of names scheduled , we care most about AXP (Thurs morning), BX (Thurs morning), GEV (Wed morning), IBM (Wed night), INTC (Thurs night), LRCX (Wed night), NOW (Wed night), PG (Fri morning), SAP (Thurs night), TEL (Wed morning), TSLA (Wed night), TXN (Wed night), and VRT (Wed morning).

Iran

• What bulls are talking about : nearly all of the headlines out of the Middle East point to an easing of acrimony and tensions as the US and Iran head toward an extension of the current ceasefire and an eventual nuclear/Hormuz deal . Trump said the war “should be ending pretty soon” and claimed that Iran has agreed to give up its enriched uranium and abandoned ambitions to build a nuclear weapon . In addition, Israel and Lebanon will observe a 10-day ceasefire.
• What bears are talking about : it’s now overwhelmingly the consensus view that the US and Iran will reach some sort of a deal to resolve their conflict and reopen Hormuz , which means headlines pointing to an agreement won’t place as much upside pressure on stocks as they did before (and the fallout from more acrimony will be significant). A durable détente isn’t guaranteed – Washington and Tehran still have some big differences to iron out , as do Israel and Lebanon . According to a Bloomberg report on Thurs, some Gulf and European governments believe it will take the US and Iran about 6 months to reach a deal , a far longer timeframe than is suggested by the current market narrative. A Reuters article highlighted the negative impact to US munitions supplies from the war , which will force the administration to delay certain weapons deliveries to Europe . Even if a deal is reached , it won’t erase the fallout from the war – the stagflationary effects of Epic Fury will be with markets for a while. The big price increase announced from PPG Wed night won’t be an isolated incident – companies across the board will be forced to adjust pricing given what’s happening in commodities, and ~$80-95 is likely to be the “new normal” for Brent vs. ~$60-70 before the war.

us_macro_section:
US macro

• US gov’t at risk of losing its status as the lowest-cost USD borrower as global investors grow wary of Trump’s volatility ( FT )
• The US economy continues to grapple with the Big 3 supply shocks of the Trump 2.0 era (tariffs, immigration, and Iran). Bessent earlier in the week said tariff rates should be back to pre-SCOTUS levels by the summer . The Fed isn’t likely to provide much support as elevated inflation keeps the Funds Rate unchanged for the foreseeable future while the pace of asset purchases is set to slump significantly (and Warsh’s long-term plans for balance sheet shrinkage remain an overhang).

Washington

• Oil tankers are rushing to the US from around the world to buy American energy products amid ongoing disruption in the Middle East ( Nikkei )
• White House officials urge the oil/gas industry to increase output to help offset the Iran war fallout ( Bloomberg )
• Oil and gas executives urge the White House not to allow Iran to charge a toll on Hormuz traffic ( FT )
• White House will begin attempting to collect delinquent student loans as soon as this summer ( Politico )
• Trump is considering appointing Michael Murray to lead antitrust at the DOJ ( Bloomberg )
• Trump plans to nominate Cameron Hamilton to run FEMA (he was pushed out as acting leader nearly a year ago) ( Politico )
• Trump will nominate Dr. Erica Schwartz to lead CDC (she was the deputy surgeon general during Trump’s first term) ( NBC News )
• US school chronic absenteeism remains extremely elevated at nearly 25% ( FT )
• Florida’s lack of private schools has prompted some billionaires to build their own ( WSJ )

Financials

• Banks face growing cybersecurity threats from sophisticated new AI models like Anthropic’s Claude Mythos ( FT )

Industrials

• AA (Alcoa) reported a shortfall in Q1 on EPS (1.40 vs. the Street 1.57), EBITDA ($595 MM vs. the Street $665 MM), and sales ($3.193 B vs. the Street $3.26 B), w/mgmt. calling out headwinds from the Middle East war and Cyclone Narelle . The prior full-year guidance for aluminum and alumina production and shipments is unchanged .
• Alstom shares collapse in European trading after the company a mixed earnings update , with strong orders/demand but poor execution , which hurt margins (adjusted margins were 6%, about 100bp below guidance), and the outlook for margins/cash flow was weak (mgmt. is withdrawing the prior 3-year free cash flow goal of EU1.5 B and won’t meet its 8-10% margin goal by F26/27) ( Alstom )
• BA (Boeing) is hiring factory workers at the fastest pace in years as it looks to increase production and replace retirees ( Reuters )
• Gerresheimer has rejected a takeout bid from SLGN (Silgan) , and talks are no longer occurring between the two firms ( Reuters )
• KNX (Knight-Swift) warned that EPS in Q1 will come in at 9c (a large miss vs. the Street’s 26c forecast) due to a number of headwinds (the company calls out ~20c worth of items in the press release), but the Q2 guidance is essentially inline (they see EPS in Q2 of 45-49c vs. the Street 48c), and the macro qualitative commentary is bullish . Mgmt. said freight conditions improved exiting Q1 (they echo what JBHT said Wed night about the TL market tightening because of reduced capacity). “All things considered, we are more optimistic about the earnings opportunity for our businesses over the next several quarters than we were three months ago”. Bottom Line : the Q1 EPS update looks bad, but the overall message from KNX is net positive, and it seems like the trucking industry is evolving for the better thanks to a sharp reduction in capacity.

• Data center delays : data center construction in the US is falling behind schedule due to opposition from local governments along with shortages (of labor, power, and equipment) ( FT )
• Ericsson reported a shortfall in Q1 sales (organic growth of +6% was about 180bp below the consensus due to weakness in the Americas) and EBITA margins (11.3% vs. the Street 11.5%), and the company acknowledges “ geopolitical and macroeconomic uncertainties ” along with cost pressures (“We are facing increasing input costs, especially in semiconductors, caused in part by AI demand”) ( Ericsson )
• GOOGL (Google) is in talks w/the Pentagon to deploy the Gemini model in classified settings ( The Information )
• META (Meta) is boosting the price of its VR headsets because of elevated memory expenses ( Bloomberg )
• NFLX (Netflix) reported modest sales upside in Q1 (+16.2% to $12.25 B vs. the Street $12.17 B, w/growth driven by membership increases, higher prices , and increased ad revenue ) w/ very robust free cash flow ($5.094 B vs. the Street $2.66 B, w/the beat driven in large part by the WBD termination fee), but op. margins fell slightly short (32.3% vs. the Street 32.4%), and while the prior full-year guidance is reiterated (for sales and op. margins), the Q2 forecast is below expectations (they see sales of $12.57 B vs. the Street $12.64 B w/op. margins of 32.6% vs. the Street 34.4%; the op. margin miss in Q2 is a function of higher content amortization, but mgmt. says Q2 should be the peak for this cost headwind in terms of Y/Y growth). Mgmt. said the recent price increases “have gone well” while advertising revenue is on track to hit $3 B in 2026, up 2x Y/Y. The firm will soon launch a redesigned mobile experience , including vertical video . In addition to earnings, the company said Reed Hastings will step down from the board in June to focus on his philanthropy and other pursuits. Bottom Line : this isn’t an awful report by any means (Q1 sales were ahead of plan, Q1 margins didn’t miss by that much, and the full-year guide is reiterated) and the Q2 guidance shortfall seems mostly just an issue of mis-modeling on content amortization as opposed to a sudden softening in business momentum.
• NI (Ni Source) announced a new long‑term energy agreement with Google that will support the development and operation of a large‑scale data center in northern Indiana. Ni Source also announced an expanded agreement with Amazon .
• ON (On Semi) said helium supplies won’t be a major issue for it this year despite disruptions in the Middle East ( Semafor )
• Only Fans is nearing a deal to sell a minority stake at a valuation of >$3 B ( FT )
• Open AI will spend >$20 B on AI chips from Cerebras and is set to receive warrants as part of the deal ( The Information )
• Open AI : a WSJ profile article raises questions about Sam Altman’s outside investment interests and whether they conflict with his role as Open AI’s CEO ( WSJ )
• Open AI on Thurs released a new AI model designed to help with the drug discovery process ( Bloomberg )
• SFR , the French telecoms group owned by Patrick Drahi, receives a EU20.4 B takeout bid from a consortium consisting of Bouygues, Orange, and Iliad ( FT )

energy, telecom, basic resources, and autos underperform . Alstom is tumbling nearly 30% on a neg. earnings update while Norsk Hydro falls on the underwhelming Alcoa report Thurs night. Tech is the top group in Europe and is being led higher by software/services . Stocks in Asia mostly suffered losses on Friday: TPX -1.41%, NKY -1.75%, Hang Seng -0.89%, HSCEI -0.67%, SHCOMP -0.1%, Shenzhen +0.37%, Taiwan TAIEX -0.88%, Korea KOSPI -0.55%, Australia ASX 200 -0.09%, and India is up 45-50bp. Treasuries aren’t doing much (yields are flat-to-down 1bp across the curve). The DXY is dipping 13bp. Brent is sliding 200bp (to ~$97.40) although remains up on WTD basis. Gold is about flat while silver climbs 150bp. Bitcoin is advancing 90bp.
• What’s happening this morning – there wasn’t anything super interesting out since the Thurs close, although Trump’s Iran rhetoric is becoming increasingly bullish/hopeful (he said Tehran agreed to hand over its enriched uranium and abandon plans for a nuclear weapon while Pakistani sources tell Reuters a MOU between the US and Iran could be signed soon ). We remain very confident that the US-Iran will stay on a path of deescalation , but as this is now the consensus perspective , resolution of the conflict won’t be as potent a source of upside risk as has been the case so far this month . Only a handful of firms had earnings out Thurs night/Fri morning, and results were generally underwhelming , at least on the surface, including AA, Alstom, Ericsson, KNX , and NFLX (although the overall KNX update was net positive , the latest encouraging data point from the trucking industry, and we would place NFLX more in the “inline” bucket rather than the “disappointing” one ).
• What we’ll be watching on Friday – the focus will be on earnings before the US open ( ALLY, ALV, FITB, RF, STT , and TFC ).
• What we’ll be watching over the weekend – Trump said the US and Iran could meet face-to-face again over the weekend , although nothing has been officially confirmed .

Iran

• What bulls are talking about : nearly all of the headlines out of the Middle East point to an easing of acrimony and tensions as the US and Iran head toward an extension of the current ceasefire and an eventual nuclear/Hormuz deal . Trump said the war “should be ending pretty soon” and claimed that Iran has agreed to give up its enriched uranium and abandoned ambitions to build a nuclear weapon . In addition, Israel and Lebanon will observe a 10-day ceasefire.
• What bears are talking about : it’s now overwhelmingly the consensus view that the US and Iran will reach some sort of a deal to resolve their conflict and reopen Hormuz , which means headlines pointing to an agreement won’t place as much upside pressure on stocks as they did before (and the fallout from more acrimony will be significant). A durable détente isn’t guaranteed – Washington and Tehran still have some big differences to iron out , as do Israel and Lebanon . According to a Bloomberg report on Thurs, some Gulf and European governments believe it will take the US and Iran about 6 months to reach a deal , a far longer timeframe than is suggested by the current market narrative. A Reuters article highlighted the negative impact to US munitions supplies from the war , which will force the administration to delay certain weapons deliveries to Europe . Even if a deal is reached , it won’t erase the fallout from the war – the stagflationary effects of Epic Fury will be with markets for a while. The big price increase announced from PPG Wed night won’t be an isolated incident – companies across the board will be forced to adjust pricing given what’s happening in commodities, and ~$80-95 is likely to be the “new normal” for Brent vs. ~$60-70 before the war.

US macro

• The US economy continues to grapple with the Big 3 supply shocks of the Trump 2.0 era (tariffs, immigration, and Iran). Bessent earlier in the week said tariff rates should be back to pre-SCOTUS levels by the summer . The Fed isn’t likely to provide much support as elevated inflation keeps the Funds Rate unchanged for the foreseeable future while the pace of asset purchases is set to slump significantly (and Warsh’s long-term plans for balance sheet shrinkage remain an overhang).

Washington

• Oil tankers are rushing to the US from around the world to buy American energy products amid ongoing disruption in the Middle East ( Nikkei )
• White House officials urge the oil/gas industry to increase output to help offset the Iran war fallout ( Bloomberg )
• Oil and gas executives urge the White House not to allow Iran to charge a toll on Hormuz traffic ( FT )
• White House will begin attempting to collect delinquent student loans as soon as this summer ( Politico )
• Trump is considering appointing Michael Murray to lead antitrust at the DOJ ( Bloomberg )
• Trump plans to nominate Cameron Hamilton to run FEMA (he was pushed out as acting leader nearly a year ago) ( Politico )
• Trump will nominate Dr. Erica Schwartz to lead CDC (she was the deputy surgeon general during Trump’s first term) ( NBC News )
• US school chronic absenteeism remains extremely elevated at nearly 25% ( FT )
• Florida’s lack of private schools has prompted some billionaires to build their own ( WSJ )
tmt_section:
TMT

• AAPL (Apple) saw i Phone shipments in China spike 20% in Q1 according to Counterpoint Research ( Reuters )
• Anthropic – the Trump administration is working to make Mythos available to federal agencies so they can reduce cybersecurity risks ( Reuters )
• Data center delays : data center construction in the US is falling behind schedule due to opposition from local governments along with shortages (of labor, power, and equipment) ( FT )
• Ericsson reported a shortfall in Q1 sales (organic growth of +6% was about 180bp below the consensus due to weakness in the Americas) and EBITA margins (11.3% vs. the Street 11.5%), and the company acknowledges “ geopolitical and macroeconomic uncertainties ” along with cost pressures (“We are facing increasing input costs, especially in semiconductors, caused in part by AI demand”) ( Ericsson )
• GOOGL (Google) is in talks w/the Pentagon to deploy the Gemini model in classified settings ( The Information )
• META (Meta) is boosting the price of its VR headsets because of elevated memory expenses ( Bloomberg )
• NFLX (Netflix) reported modest sales upside in Q1 (+16.2% to $12.25 B vs. the Street $12.17 B, w/growth driven by membership increases, higher prices , and increased ad revenue ) w/ very robust free cash flow ($5.094 B vs. the Street $2.66 B, w/the beat driven in large part by the WBD termination fee), but op. margins fell slightly short (32.3% vs. the Street 32.4%), and while the prior full-year guidance is reiterated (for sales and op. margins), the Q2 forecast is below expectations (they see sales of $12.57 B vs. the Street $12.64 B w/op. margins of 32.6% vs. the Street 34.4%; the op. margin miss in Q2 is a function of higher content amortization, but mgmt. says Q2 should be the peak for this cost headwind in terms of Y/Y growth). Mgmt. said the recent price increases “have gone well” while advertising revenue is on track to hit $3 B in 2026, up 2x Y/Y. The firm will soon launch a redesigned mobile experience , including vertical video . In addition to earnings, the company said Reed Hastings will step down from the board in June to focus on his philanthropy and other pursuits. Bottom Line : this isn’t an awful report by any means (Q1 sales were ahead of plan, Q1 margins didn’t miss by that much, and the full-year guide is reiterated) and the Q2 guidance shortfall seems mostly just an issue of mis-modeling on content amortization as opposed to a sudden softening in business momentum.
• NI (Ni Source) announced a new long‑term energy agreement with Google that will support the development and operation of a large‑scale data center in northern Indiana. Ni Source also announced an expanded agreement with Amazon .
• ON (On Semi) said helium supplies won’t be a major issue for it this year despite disruptions in the Middle East ( Semafor )
• Only Fans is nearing a deal to sell a minority stake at a valuation of >$3 B ( FT )
• Open AI will spend >$20 B on AI chips from Cerebras and is set to receive warrants as part of the deal ( The Information )
• Open AI : a WSJ profile article raises questions about Sam Altman’s outside investment interests and whether they conflict with his role as Open AI’s CEO ( WSJ )
• Open AI on Thurs released a new AI model designed to help with the drug discovery process ( Bloomberg )
• SFR , the French telecoms group owned by Patrick Drahi, receives a EU20.4 B takeout bid from a consortium consisting of Bouygues, Orange, and Iliad ( FT )
• UBER (Uber) will buy a ~4.5% stake in Delivery Hero from Prosus for EU20/shr. (a 22% premium to Delivery Hero’s average share price for the last month) ( WSJ )
